title: Houston Unhoused
text:
1. About this data
This project is a project for the Media Innovation Group at the University of Texas at Austin in collaboration with Houston Public Media.
It is a study of Houston court data showing citations given to unhoused people in downtown Houston. The data ranges from Jan. 1 2016 to Oct. 28 2025, and includes columns detailing defendant information, offense date, offense location, court judgment date, fines information and violation description. To map offense locations, addresses are geocoded using geocod.io. OpenRefine was also used to cluster people with misspelled or varied names.
John Whitmire became Houston mayor in Jan. 2024, promising to end street homelessness during his term. Therefore this summary will often compare numbers and underline differences between before and after his term began.
There are seven types of violations that have been given since 2016, all of which can be categorized into a sidewalk obstruction ordinance, civility ordinance or other. There are also four new violation types that were only introduced in 2025, three of which are sidewalk obstructions, the fourth is other.

title: Houston Unhoused
text:
2. Limitations and considerations

OpenRefine was used to cluster misspelled and varied names. Each decision was made manually based on calculated judgment calls, which may portray people with more/less citations than they actually received. It may also impact how many individual people are estimated to have received citations.
Some citations have duplicate case numbers because the second appearance tracks how the case developed. So, to confidently know that each row does represent an individual citation, an object that does not have duplicate rows will be created at the beginning of the Analysis notebook. In that object, the second appearance of each case will be kept because that provides the most updated data.

title: Houston Unhoused
text: 3. Quick facts

title: Houston Unhoused
text:
4. Summary

4.1 Citation count over time
Looking six months before Whitmire’s term began, Sept. 2025 saw the highest citation numbers in that date range, at 338. The monthly citation count from May to Sept. 2025 seems to be on an upward climb.
@ insert figure 2.2.1.1
Looking back even further, Sept. 2025 had the highest citation numbers since since June 2021. However, to put that in the larger context, Sept. 2025 only had 42.4% of the Jan. 2021 citation count, which is the highest citation month in this data set.
@ insert figure 2.2.2.1
See more about citation counts over time in section 2 of the analysis notebook.


4.2 Violation types
There are seven types of violations that have been given since 2016. Here is what they mean and their frequency: @summarize this and link to the more detailed information.

CC753 has 21 citations, a street vendor ordinance

Selling goods in public area: STREET VENDOR (EXPOSE FOR SALE) (SELL) GOODS, TO-WIT:…ON A PUBLIC (SIDEWALK) (STREET) (PROPERTY)

CC921 has 477 citations, a sidewalk obstruction ordinance

Sidewalk obstruction: OBSTRUCT SIDEWALK BY (PLACING/DEPOSITING/PERMIT PERSON UNDER CONTROL) OBJECT (BOX/MAT./VEH./ETC.)

CC940 has 15,038 citations, a civility ordinance

Sidewalk obstruction: SIT/LIE DOWN ON (BLANKET/STOOL) PLACED ON SIDEWALK B/W 7:00 AM AND 11:00 PM IN A PROHIBITED AREA

CC941 has 13,591 citations, a civility ordinance

Sidewalk obstruction: PLACE (BED MAT./PERSONAL POSS.) ON SIDEWALK B/W THE HRS OF 7:00 A.M. & 11:00P.M. IN A PROHIBITED AREA

CC942 has 97 citations, a sidewalk obstruction ordinance

Sidewalk obstruction: IMPAIR OR OBSTRUCT SIDEWALK WITHOUT A PERMIT

CC943 has 5 citations, a sidewalk obstruction ordinance

Sidewalk obstruction: IMPAIR OR OBSTRUCT SIDEWALK BEYOND SCOPE OF PERMIT

CC948 has 229 citations, a sidewalk obstruction ordinance

Sidewalk obstruction: PLACE OR ALLOW OBSTRUCTION ON SIDEWALK

CC956 has 1 citation, a street vendor ordinance

EXPOSE FOR SALE MERCHANDISE, TO-WIT:…ON A PUBLIC (SIDEWALK) (STREET) (ESPLANADE) (PROPERTY)


CC940 and CC941 monthly citation numbers follow similar trends throughout the year (Analysis 3.3.2), suggesting they are given out at the same rate.
After Whitmire introduced three new sidewalk obstruction ordinances this year, Sept. 2025 had highest sidewalk obstruction citations per month in this data set. The 127 citations made up around 38% of all citations given that month.
@ insert figure 3.3.2

text:
1.3 Merging misspelled columns

1.3.1 Evaluating columns
Looking at columns to evaluate which function to use.

unhoused_dates |> 
  select(defendant_address, defendent_address)


  





1.3.2 Merging
Merging defendant_address and defendent_address into one column.

unhoused_coalesce <- unhoused_dates |>
  mutate(
    defendant_address = coalesce(defendant_address, defendent_address) # this function is able to generate a complete vector from partially missing pieces
  ) |> 
  select(!defendent_address)

unhoused_to_refine <- unhoused_selected |>
  arrange(desc(data_origin)) |> # distinct() keeps the first row between two duplicates, so arranging it in descending order by data_origin, it makes sure we keep the most recent row
  distinct(across(!c(
    data_origin, 
    defendant_address)),
    .keep_all = TRUE)

text:
6. Grouping by dates

unhoused_grouped_dates <- unhoused_open_refined |> 
  mutate(
    off_yr = year(offense_date), # offense year
    off_mo = month(offense_date), # offense month in numbers
    off_mo_label = month(offense_date, label = TRUE), # offense month written
    off_yday = yday(offense_date), # offense day/365
    off_floor_mo = floor_date(offense_date, unit = "month"), # floor month
    off_floor_yr = floor_date(offense_date, "year"), # floor year
    jud_yr = year(judgment_date),
    jud_mo = month(judgment_date),
    jud_mo_label = month(judgment_date, label = TRUE),
    jud_yday = yday(judgment_date),
    jud_floor_mo = floor_date(judgment_date, unit = "month"),
    jud_floor_yr = floor_date(judgment_date, "year")
  )

title: Cleaning
text:
7. Creating and selecting columns

7.1 Whitmire flag
I chose to separate these columns because there may be cases where a citation was given before Whitmire’s term began, but didn’t receive a judgment until after.

7.1.2 Offense date
Creating a column indicating whether an offense occurred before or after Whitmire’s term began.

unhoused_whitmire_offense <- unhoused_grouped_dates |>
  mutate(
    whitmire_offense = if_else( #if_else function creates a new column based on its argument. it instructs that if the argument is true, use "before," if it's false, use "after." this makes sure we include Jan. 1 2024 in "after"
      offense_date < as.Date("2024-01-01"),
      "BEFORE WHITMIRE",
      "AFTER WHITMIRE"
    )
  )

7.2 Violation tag
Making a column to tag whether a citation falls under a civility ordinance vs sidewalk obstruction.

  mutate(
    citation_type = ifelse(
      violation_code == "CC940" | violation_code == "CC941", "CIVILITY ORDINANCE",
      ifelse(
        violation_code == "CC921" | violation_code == "CC942" | violation_code == "CC943" | violation_code == "CC948", "SIDEWALK OBSTRUCTION ORDINANCE",
        ifelse(
          violation_code == "CC753" | violation_code == "CC956", "OTHER",
          "OTHER"
  ))))

title: Analysis
text:
Create an object containing only rows with only its most recent case number.
Investigate citation numbers over time

What is the year with the most citations?
What is the month with the most citations?
What is the average number of citations for each month since 2016?

Investigate most common violation types

What are the most common violation types of all time?
What are the most common violation types by year?

Judgement and dispositions

What is the most common judgement?
What is the most common disposition?
How many guilty cases per year?
What is the month with the most guilty dispositions and highest percentage?
What is the average percentage of guilty dispositions for each month from 2016-2024?

Top people

Who has received the most citations? How many people received multiple citations/fines?
Individual fine statistics A. Who are the most heavily fined people? B. How much of those fines got dismissed? C. How much did they pay? D. How much do they still have due? E. Who has paid the highest amount of dues?
General fine statistics A. How many people still owe fines to the city? B. How many people have paid their fines? C. How many people have had their fines dismissed?

Fines

How many distinct people are there who were fined?
How many people still owe fines to the city? How much is the city owed in total?
How many people have paid some/all of their fines?
How many people have had some/all of their fines dismissed?

Jail time

How many jail judgments have been given?
How many individual people have been jailed? How many have been jailed more than once?

Individual people analysis

Kevin Shakesnider received the highest jail judgments, let’s visualize that.
Patricia Norris has the highest citation count, let’s visualize that.

Mapping analysis

To add perhaps: 5. Dismissal impacts i) Do dismissal rates change over time ii) How many people with multiple citations also had their fines dismissed? iii) Does time between citation date and judgment date impact dismissal? 6. Other i) How many individual people are there ii) Do people who receive time also receive fines?

title: Analysis
text:
1. Creating distinct object
Right now, some rows have duplicate case numbers because the second appearance tracks how the case developed. So, to confidently know that each row does represent an individual citation, I will create an object that does not have duplicate rows. I will be keeping the second appearance because that provides the most updated data.


Expand this to see code
unhoused_distinct <- unhoused |> 
  group_by(case_number) |>  
  arrange(desc(judgment_date)) |> 
  slice(1) |>
  ungroup() |> 
  mutate(off_mo_label = factor(off_mo_label, levels = month.abb, ordered = TRUE), # slicing changed the mo_label columns from <ord> to <chr>, this fixes that
         jud_mo_label = factor(jud_mo_label, levels = month.abb, ordered = TRUE)
        )

title: Analysis
text:
2. Citation numbers over time
Looking at how individual citation numbers have changed over time.

2.1. Year with most citations
What is the year with the most citations?

2.1.1 Finding year most citations


Expand this to see code
unhoused_yearly <- unhoused_distinct |> # this ensures every row counts as an individual citation
  #filter(off_yr < 2025) |> # excluding 2025 because it's not a complete year
  group_by(off_floor_yr, off_yr, whitmire_offense) |> 
  summarize(yearly_offenses = n()) |> 
  arrange(off_floor_yr) 


`summarise()` has grouped output by 'off_floor_yr', 'off_yr'. You can override
using the `.groups` argument.


Expand this to see code
unhoused_yearly



  





2.1.2 Plotting
Plotting yearly offenses as an interactive bar chart.


Expand this to see code
yearly_ggplot <- ggplot(
  unhoused_yearly,
  aes(x = off_floor_yr, y = yearly_offenses)
  ) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(
    title = "Yearly citations fluctuate from 2016 to 2024 ",
    caption = "By Layla Dajani",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Offenses per year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

yearly_ggplot |> 
  ggplotly()








2.1.3 Data takeaways

The year with the highest number of citations is 2016 with 5800, followed by 2020 with 5011. The year with the lowest number of citations is 2018, at 1385.

2019 saw 1815 citations and 2020 had 5011 – that is roughly a 176% increase in one year. 2022 had 2007 citations, which is a roughly 60% decrease from 2020 to 2022.




2.2 Highest citation month

2.2.1 Jul. 2023 to Sept. 2025
What’s the month with the most citations from Jul. 2023 to Jan. 2025?


Expand this to see code
monthly_citations <- unhoused_distinct |> 
  filter(offense_date >= "2023-07-01", # i'm looking 6 months before Whitmire's term started to see if there is an uptick in citations after Jan. 2024.
         offense_date <= "2025-09-30") |> 
  group_by(off_floor_mo, whitmire_offense) |> # adding Whitmire offense for when i plot
  summarise(
    yr_mo_off = n()) |> 
  arrange(off_floor_mo)


`summarise()` has grouped output by 'off_floor_mo'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.


Expand this to see code
monthly_citations



  




2.2.1.1 Plotting - for summary
@ Include in summary.


Expand this to see code
monthly_citations_plot <- ggplot(monthly_citations, 
       aes(x = off_floor_mo, 
           y = yr_mo_off, 
           fill = whitmire_offense)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ymd("2024-01-01"), 
             linetype = "dashed", 
             color = "grey40") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("BEFORE WHITMIRE" = "blue", 
                                "AFTER WHITMIRE" = "red")
                     ) +
  labs(
    title = "June to Sept. 2025 indicate an upward climb compared to previous months",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Citations per month",
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

monthly_citations_plot |> 
  ggplotly()


Warning: No shared levels found between `names(values)` of the manual scale and the
data's colour values.








2.2.1.2 Data takeaways

Since 2016:

The month with the highest citation number since 2016 is Jan. 2021, with 797 citations. The month with the lowest citation number since 2016 is March 2022 with 29 citations.

Since July 2023, 6 months before Whitmire’s term began:

The month with the highest citation number since July 2023 is Sep. 2025 with 338 citations, followed by Aug. 2025 with 321 citations. The month with the lowest citation number since July 2023 is July 2023, with 66 citations. This is followed by May 2023 with 87 citations.

    yr_mo_off = n()) |>

2.3 Avg monthly citation


2.3.1 Creating object
What is the average monthly citation count for each month from 2016 to 2024?


Expand this to see code
mo_avg_citations <- unhoused_distinct |> 
  filter(off_yr < 2025) |> 
  group_by(off_yr,
           off_floor_mo,
           off_mo_label) |> 
  summarise(yr_mo_off = n()) |> 
  group_by(off_mo_label) |> 
  summarise(avg_mo_off = round(mean(yr_mo_off), 1)) |> 
  arrange(off_mo_label)


`summarise()` has grouped output by 'off_yr', 'off_floor_mo'. You can override
using the `.groups` argument.

  mo_avg_citations,
  aes(x = off_mo_label, y = avg_mo_off, group = 1)
  ) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly citations averaged from Jan. 2016 to Dec. 2024",
    subtitle = str_wrap(""),
    caption = "Graphic by Layla Dajani",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Offenses per month",
  ) +

2.3.2 Data takeaways

May and June seem to be the months that draw the most citaitons. Surges in specific years could be driving the averages higher in certain months. For instance, January 2021 had the highest citations (787) of any month since 2016.

title: Analysis
text:
3. Violation types

3.1 Most common
Finding the most common violation types.


Expand this to see code
violation_count <- unhoused_distinct |> 
  group_by(violation_code, violation_description) |> 
  summarise(violations = n()) |> 
  arrange(desc(violations))


`summarise()` has grouped output by 'violation_code'. You can override using
the `.groups` argument.


Expand this to see code
violation_count



  




3.1.1 Data takeaways
There are seven types of violations that have been given since 2016. Here is what they mean and their frequency:

CC753 has 21 citations, a street vendor ordinance

Selling goods in public area: STREET VENDOR (EXPOSE FOR SALE) (SELL) GOODS, TO-WIT:…ON A PUBLIC (SIDEWALK) (STREET) (PROPERTY)

CC921 has 477 citations, a sidewalk obstruction ordinance

Sidewalk obstruction: OBSTRUCT SIDEWALK BY (PLACING/DEPOSITING/PERMIT PERSON UNDER CONTROL) OBJECT (BOX/MAT./VEH./ETC.)

CC940 has 15,038 citations, a civility ordinance

Sidewalk obstruction: SIT/LIE DOWN ON (BLANKET/STOOL) PLACED ON SIDEWALK B/W 7:00 AM AND 11:00 PM IN A PROHIBITED AREA

CC941 has 13,591 citations, a civility ordinance

Sidewalk obstruction: PLACE (BED MAT./PERSONAL POSS.) ON SIDEWALK B/W THE HRS OF 7:00 A.M. & 11:00P.M. IN A PROHIBITED AREA

CC942 has 97 citations, a sidewalk obstruction ordinance

Sidewalk obstruction: IMPAIR OR OBSTRUCT SIDEWALK WITHOUT A PERMIT

CC943 has 5 citations, a sidewalk obstruction ordinance

Sidewalk obstruction: IMPAIR OR OBSTRUCT SIDEWALK BEYOND SCOPE OF PERMIT

CC948 has 229 citations, a sidewalk obstruction ordinance

Sidewalk obstruction: PLACE OR ALLOW OBSTRUCTION ON SIDEWALK

CC956 has 1 citation, a street vendor ordinance

EXPOSE FOR SALE MERCHANDISE, TO-WIT:…ON A PUBLIC (SIDEWALK) (STREET) (ESPLANADE) (PROPERTY)


The most common citation since 2016 is CC940 with 15,038 offenses, with CC941 as a runner-up at 13,591 offenses. The next most common is CC291 with only 477 offenses.



3.2 Most common by year plot


Expand this to see code
unhoused_distinct |> 
  #filter(offense_date <= "2025-01-01") |> # we don't have a complete 2025 year
  group_by(off_floor_yr,
           violation_code)|> 
  summarise(violations = n()) |> 
  arrange(desc(off_floor_yr)) |> 
  ggplot(
    aes(x = off_floor_yr, y = violations, color = violation_code)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(
    title = "Number of citations by year broken down by violation code from Jan. 2016 to Dec. 2024",
    subtitle = "",
    caption = "By Layla Dajani",
    fill = "Citations",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Citations per year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()


`summarise()` has grouped output by 'off_floor_yr'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.
Ignoring unknown labels: • fill : "Citations"










3.2.1 Data takeaways

All 21 of the CC753 citations, all 97 of CC942 citations, all five of CC943 citations and all 229 of CC948 citations have been given in 2025. CC940 and CC941 seem to follow very similar trends. Note that 2025 is an incomplete year.




3.3 Most common by month

3.3.1 Making object


Expand this to see code
types_by_month <- unhoused_distinct |>
  filter(offense_date <= "2025-09-30") |> 
  group_by(off_floor_mo, 
           violation_code,
           ordinance_type) |>
  summarise(citation_count = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(off_floor_mo)

types_by_month



  





3.3.2 Plotting


Expand this to see code
types_by_month_plot <- ggplot(
  types_by_month,
  aes(x = off_floor_mo, y = citation_count, color = violation_code)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(size = 0.4) +
  labs(
    title = "Number of citations by year broken down by violation code from Jan. 2016 to Dec. 2024",
    subtitle = "",
    caption = "By Layla Dajani",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Citations per year"
      ) +
theme_minimal()

types_by_month_plot |> 
  ggplotly() #@ add new column with descriptors 1 word









3.3.3 Data takeaways

This further suggests CC940 and CC941 lines follow similar patterns, indicating they have been given out at the same rate. But starting July 2025, when Whitmire introduced new ordinances, there became less CC941 citations relative to CC940 numbers, while new ordinance citations increased.



3.3 Ordinance types over time

3.3.1 Making object
Grouping by ordinance type and plotting over time. @ fix x axis stops


Expand this to see code
types_over_time <- unhoused_distinct |>
  filter(offense_date >= "2023-07-01",
         offense_date <= "2025-09-30") |> 
  group_by(off_floor_mo, 
           ordinance_type) |>
  summarise(citation_count = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(off_floor_mo)

types_over_time

  types_over_time,
  aes(x = off_floor_mo, 
      y = citation_count, 
      fill = ordinance_type
      )) +
  geom_col(position = "stack") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ymd("2024-01-01"), 
             linetype = "dashed", 
             color = "grey35") +

    x = "Date",

types_over_time_plot |>

3.3.3 Data takeaways
Sept. 2025 had highest sidewalk obstruction citations per month since Jan. 2016. The 127 citations made up around 38% of the total citations from that month.

title: Analysis
text:
4. Judgment and dispositions

4.1 Most common judgment
What is the most common specific judgment?


Expand this to see code
unhoused |> # I'm not using unhoused_distinct here because i want to see the true judgment stats, even if there are repeated cases, those repeats each received their own judgment in court.
  group_by(judgment) |> 
  summarise(judgment_count = n()) |> 
  arrange(desc(judgment_count))



  



What is the most common refined judgment?


Expand this to see code
unhoused |>
  group_by(judgment_refined) |> 
  summarise(judgment_count = n()) |>
  arrange(desc(judgment_count))



  




4.1.1 Data takeaways

The most common judgment is “CASE DISMISSED - OFFICER NOT PRESENT” with 8,423 counts, followed by “CREDIT TIME SERVED” with 5,422 counts and “FAILURE TO APPEAR WARRANT (FTA)” with 5,103 counts.




4.2 Most common disposition
What is the most common disposition?


Expand this to see code
unhoused |> 
  group_by(disposition) |> 
  summarise(disposition_count = n()) |> 
  arrange(desc(disposition_count))



  




4.2.1 Data takeaways

The most common disposition type is “UNDISPOSED” with 14,295 counts, followed by “DISMISSED AT TRIAL” with 9,378 counts and “GUILTY TRIAL BY JUDGE” with 5,653 counts.




4.3 Yearly guilty cases
What is the year with the most guilty dispositions?


Expand this to see code
unhoused |> 
  filter(off_yr < 2025,
         disposition == "GUILTY TRIAL BY JUDGE") |> 
  group_by(off_yr) |> 
  summarize(yearly_guilty = n()) |> 
  arrange((off_yr)) 



  




4.3.1 Data takeaways

2018 is the year with the lowest number of guilty dispositions at 50 dispositions.
2016 is the year with the highest number of guilty dispositions at 1951 dispositions, followed by 2021 with 931.




4.4 Highest guilty month
Of all the individual months each year, what is the month with the most guilty dispositions percentage relative to all dispositions that month?


Expand this to see code
unhoused |> 
  filter(judgment_date >= "2023-07-01") |> # i'm looking 6 months before Whitmire's term started to see if there is an uptick in citations after Jan. 2024.
  group_by(jud_yr,
           jud_mo_label) |> 
  summarise(guilty = sum(disposition == "GUILTY TRIAL BY JUDGE"),
            total = n(),
            guilty_pct = round((guilty/total) * 100, 2)
            ) |> 
  arrange(desc(guilty))


`summarise()` has grouped output by 'jud_yr'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.



  




4.4.1 Data takeaways

Since 2016:

Percentages: The month with the highest percentage of guilty dispositions is Mar. 2021 at 78.82% and 160/203 guilty dispositions. 33 months had no guilty dispositions, many of which are in 2018-2020. The month with the lowest percentage of guilty dispositions that is above 0 is Oct. 2018 at 0.75% and 1/134 guilty dispositions, closely followed by Oct. 2020 at 0.92% and 3/327 guilty dispositions.
Dispositions: The month with the highest number of guilty dispositions since 2016 is Jun. 2016, with 197 dispositions, followed closely by Apr. 2016 with 196. The month with the lowest guilty dispositions since 2016 that is above zero is is Oct. 2018 with 1 disposition.

Since July 2023, 6 months before Whitmire’s term began:

Percentages: The month with the highest percentage of guilty dispositions since July 2023 is Aug. 2023 at 40.32% and 75/186 guilty dispositions. All months since July 2023 have had above 0 guilty dispositions. The month with the lowest percentage of guilty dispositions since July 2023 is Jan. 2025 at 3.55% and 5/141 guilty dispositions, followed by May 2024 at 5.19% and 40/771 guilty dispositions.
Dispositions: The month with the highest guilty dispositions since July 2023 is Aug. 2025 with 139 guilty dispositions, followed by Apr. 2025 with 113. The month with the lowest guilty dispositions since July 2023 is Jan. 2025 with 5 guilty dispositions, followed by Nov. 2024 with 14.





4.5 Avg monthly guilty
What is the average percentage of guilty dispositions for each month since 2016?


Expand this to see code
unhoused |> 
  filter(judgment_date < "2025-01-01") |> # 2025 is not a complete year so that would skew later months' results
  group_by(jud_yr,
           jud_mo_label) |>
  summarise(guilty = sum(disposition == "GUILTY TRIAL BY JUDGE"),
            total = n(),
            guilty_pct = (guilty/total) * 100
            ) |>
  group_by(jud_mo_label) |>
  summarise(avg_guilty_pct = round(mean(guilty_pct), 2)) |> 
  arrange(avg_guilty_pct)


`summarise()` has grouped output by 'jud_yr'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.



  




4.5.1 Data takeaways

Looking at an average of every month from 2016 to 2024, the month with the lowest average percentage of guilty dispositions is September at 14.02%, followed by December at 14.12%.
Looking at an average of every month from 2016 to 2024, the month with the highest average percentage of guilty dispositions is March at 24.86%, followed by January at 21.66%.

title: Analysis
text:
5. Top people

5.1 Distinct people
How many distinct people have received a citation?


Expand this to see code
unhoused_distinct |> 
  summarise(people_count = n_distinct(refined_name))



  




5.1.1 Data takeaway

There are 4597 distinct people who have received a citation since 2016.




5.2 Most citations
Who has received the most citations?


Expand this to see code
unhoused_distinct |> 
  group_by(refined_name) |> 
  summarise(citation_count = n(),
            first_citation = min(offense_date),
            last_citation = max(offense_date),
            ) |> 
  arrange(desc(citation_count)) |> 
  filter(citation_count >= 100)



  




5.2.1 Data takeaways

There are 26 people who have received at least 100 total citations.
The person with the most citations is Patricia Norris, who has received 794 total citations, all given between October 2017 and May 2025.
The person with the second most citations is Forest H Smith, who has received 336 total citations, all given between August 2020 and October 2025




5.3 Most fines and dues
Who are the most heavily fined people? How much of those fines got dismissed? How much did they pay? How much do they still have due? Who has paid the highest amount of dues?


Expand this to see code
individual_fines <- unhoused_distinct |>
  group_by(refined_name) |>
  summarise(total_fined = sum(amount_fined, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_fines_dismissed = sum(fines_dismissed, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_fines_paid = sum(fines_paid, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_fines_due = sum(fines_due, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(total_fines_paid))

individual_fines



  




5.3.1 Data takeaways

Patricia Norris has been charged the highest total fines at around $199,123. She hasn’t paid any of her fines, but has had about $149,118 dismissed, which leaves about $50,006 that she still owes.
Forest H Smith is the second most fined person, with around $83,238 total fines. He has also never paid any of his fines, but has had roughly $9,584 dismissed, leaving around $73,654 that he still owes.
Kevin P Shakesnider is the person who has paid the highest amount of fines. He has paid about $28,684 of his total $44,453, and has had roughly $10,438 dismissed. As of October 2025, he still owes about $5,331.

How many people have had some/all of their fines dismissed?

            owed_fines_total = sum(total_fines_due)
            )



  





6.3 Fines paid

How many people have paid at least some of their fines?

individual_fines |> 
  filter(total_fines_paid > 0) |> 
  summarise(people_paid_some_fines = n(),
            paid_fines_total = sum(total_fines_paid) 
            )



  





6.3.2 All fines paid - no dismissals
How many people have paid all of their fines with no help from dismissals?

individual_fines |>

  summarise(people_paid_all_fines = n(),
            paid_fines_total = sum(total_fines_paid) 
            )



  






6.4 Fines dismissed
How many people have had some/all of their fines dismissed?


Expand this to see code
individual_fines |> 
  filter(total_fines_dismissed > 0,
         total_fines_dismissed == total_fined) |> 
  summarise(individual_fines_dismissed = n()
            )



  





6.5 Data takeaways

There are 4371 distinct people who have received a fine with their citation since 2016.
Of the 4371 distinct people who have received a fine with their citation, 2889 of them still owe fines. The sum of total fines still owed to the city is $2,919,485.
Of the 4371 distinct people who have received a fine with their citation, 1222 of them have paid at least some of their fines. 789 of those people have paid off all their fines without any help from dismissals. The total amount of fines paid since 2016 is about $1,485,249.
Of the 4371 distinct people who have received a fine with their citation, 1459 people have had at least some of their fines dismissed, and 703 of those 1459 people had all of their fines dismissed.

9.6 Plotting

9.6.1 General ggplot
Plotting a stagnant heat map to show each district’s citations.


Expand this to see code
ggplot(unhoused_to_map) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = citation_count), color = "white", size = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", na.value = "grey90") +
  labs(
    title = "Citations given to unhoused people by Houston City Council district",
    caption = "By Layla Dajani",
    fill = "Citations",
    x = "Longitude",
    y = "Latitude"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()











9.6.1 General mapview
Plotting an interactive heat map to show each district’s citations.

  zcol = "citation_count",
